petitioner_background: The petitioner is a music composer seeking classification as an individual of extraordinary ability under EB-1A (INA § 203(b)(1)(A)). He composed original music scores for films including two works directed by W-S- that streamed on Netflix. The Nebraska Service Center director denied the petition, and the matter came before the AAO on appeal.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of his 'Best Original Song' award at the [redacted] Awards monthly international film festival. His appellate submission included material from the Awards website listing its 2023 winners, 35 award categories, and rules of entry. He also submitted promotional material for the Awards on FilmFreeway (a website filmmakers use to submit films to hundreds of film festivals globally), which included a 'Submit Now' button and listed the Awards' categories, rules, terms of entry, and user reviews. The petitioner argued that the Awards is an international film competition recognizing excellence in independent filmmaking and aims to support emerging filmmakers by providing a platform for visibility and recognition.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the information from both the Awards and FilmFreeway websites insufficient to show that the 'Best Original Song' award garnered sufficient recognition beyond the context of the monthly film festival where it was presented. The AAO noted the Awards' rules stated entry fees are non-refundable, multiple entries are allowed, and filmmakers can submit their work as many times as they want. The petitioner did not demonstrate the significance of his specific award in the field of endeavor or that it received media coverage or other attention rising to the level of national or international recognition. Without further evidence regarding its national or international significance, the petitioner failed to establish the award was a nationally or internationally recognized prize for excellence in the field.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The decision does not detail the specific published material evidence, as this criterion was found met by the Director and not contested on appeal.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director determined the petitioner fulfilled the published material criterion at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(iii), and this finding was not disturbed on appeal. The AAO acknowledged that the petitioner meets this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted Netflix webpages pertaining to two films accompanied by 'Full Cast & Crew' information from IMDb.com listing both films' 'Music by [the Petitioner].' With the appeal, he provided an October 2024 letter from W-S-, film director, stating that the petitioner worked as the composer for his films (2021) and (2023), and was responsible for composing the original music score which significantly contributed to the atmosphere, emotional depth, and storytelling of the films. W-S- asserted the petitioner's work contributed to the films' 'exhibition and reception, both at Netflix and across public platforms.' The petitioner also submitted an article entitled 'How Netflix Expanded to 190 Countries in 7 Years' discussing the streaming platform's global growth and market reach, and two online reviews of one film posted in July and September of 2023 on the film's IMDb.com webpage, one of which complimented the film's music. The petitioner argued that streaming platforms like Netflix serve as prominent public exhibition venues, reaching millions of viewers globally as modern equivalents to traditional theaters and galleries.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO relied on Merriam-Webster's definition of 'display' as 'to place or spread (something) for people to see' and 'to serve as a place for displaying (something).' The AAO concluded that the petitioner's music work for the films is an artistic component that is heard but not seen or displayed, so he did not display his musical contributions consistent with the plain language of the regulation. The AAO applied the ordinary, common meaning of 'display' citing Perrin v. United States. Additionally, the AAO found the petitioner had not shown his specific work product was being displayed as the subject of an artistic exhibition or showcase on the Netflix streaming platform.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The decision does not detail the specific evidence, as this criterion was found met by the Director and not contested on appeal.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director determined the petitioner fulfilled the leading or critical role criterion at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(viii), and this finding was not disturbed on appeal. The AAO acknowledged that the petitioner meets this criterion.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal because the petitioner established only two of the required three initial evidentiary criteria (published material and leading/critical role), failing on both the awards and display criteria. Because the inability to meet three initial criteria was dispositive, the AAO reserved the final merits determination under Kazarian. Nevertheless, the AAO reviewed the record in the aggregate and concluded it did not support a finding that the petitioner had achieved the sustained national or international acclaim required. The AAO emphasized that EB-1A is a highly restrictive classification intended for individuals already at the top of their field, not those progressing toward the top, and the petitioner had not shown his work was indicative of a 'career of acclaimed work in the field' as contemplated by Congress.
legal_citations: Matter of Christo's, Inc., 26 I&N Dec. 537 (AAO 2015)
Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011)
Hamal v. Dep't of Homeland Sec. (Hamal II), No. 19-cv-2534, 2021 WL 2338316 (D.D.C. June 8, 2021)
Hamal v. Dep't of Homeland Sec. (Hamal I), No. 19-cv-2534, 2020 WL 2934954 (D.D.C. June 3, 2020)
Lee v. Ziglar, 237 F. Supp. 2d 914 (N.D. Ill. 2002)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The AAO noted that the petitioner did not contest the Director's findings on the judging criterion at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(iv), so that issue was deemed abandoned and waived on appeal. Similarly, the petitioner did not contest findings on other previously claimed awards or other display evidence, and those issues were treated as waived. No fraud or misrepresentation findings were made.
